news.title: Exclusive-China's DeepSeek developing its own AI chip, sources say
news.summary: July 7 () - Chinese startup DeepSeek is developing its own AI chip, according to three people familiar with the matter, a push that could reduce its reliance on Nvidia and Huawei chips, which it has depended on to train and run its globally popular models. The chip is designed for ‌inference — the stage of AI computing in which a trained model generates responses for users — rather than for training new models, the sources said.

news.title: What to Know as SpaceX Joins the Nasdaq-100
news.summary: SpaceX officially joins the Nasdaq-100 today, and investors holding some funds tied to the index will end up exposed, whether they like it or not.  Mutual and exchange-traded funds with $800 billion in assets under management are expected to buy SpaceX shares in order to mirror the index’s performance.  SpaceX will be a small component in the Nasdaq-100, for now.

news.title: Tech Weekly: Tesla's surge, robot footballers target glory
news.summary:
STORY: From Tesla driving up sales to robot footballers competing in their own competition...

This is Tech Weekly.

:: Tech Weekly

Tesla beat Wall Street estimates when it posted record second-quarter deliveries.

The electric vehicle maker delivered more than 480,000 vehicles between April and June - up about a quarter from a year ago.

The results were partly driven by a rebound in Europe.

China was another bright spot, although demand was still under pressure in the U.S.

The World Cup witnessed a technological first during Norway's 2-1 win over Brazil in the Round of 16. 

An advanced humanoid robot called Atlas delivered the match ball to the referee at half-time.

The robot was developed by U.S. firm Boston Dynamics and presented by tournament sponsor Hyundai Motor.

Atlas can impersonate famous goal celebrations, including Norway striker Erling Haaland's meditation pose.

While Atlas delivered a ball, robots in South Korea showed they could actually play soccer.

They competed at the finals of RoboCup 2026 in Incheon - a major robotics and AI competition.

Organisers said more than 3,000 competitors from 45 countries took part, and many teams reportedly used platforms developed by China's Booster Robotics.

The robots stumbled around and collided with rivals but got back up to keep playing, with Team Hephaestus of Tsinghua University winning the contest.

Google lost its long fight against a record $4 billion plus fine imposed by the EU eight years ago.

The European Commission had punished the tech giant for its dealings with phone manufacturers.

The phone makers were forced to pre-install Google Search, the Chrome browser and the Google Play app store on their Android devices - and prevented from using rival systems.

A Google spokesperson said the judgment didn't account for its efforts to make sure Android is open, interoperable and free.

And customers at the Lounge X coffee shop in Seoul are now assisted by a robot named Baris.

Its a technological sight which has become more common across South Korea.

Lounge X CEO Kim Dongjin said the firm turned to robots and self-service to overcome rising labor costs.

Kim says his company runs eight such unstaffed 24-hour coffee shops across South Korea, while four stores are still operated by humans.

news.title: SpaceX Has Joined the Nasdaq-100, Now Brace for Volatility
news.summary: SpaceX  trading on Tuesday won’t have much to do with space or AI fundamentals.  Elon Musk’s AI and space company is now in the Nasdaq-100, one of the best-known large-cap growth indexes that tracks the largest nonfinancial companies listed on Nasdaq.  Being fast-tracked into the index reflects just how important privately held frontier AI companies, including OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX before its June IPO, have become to the entire stock market.

news.title: China-based Apple supplier Luxshare sets Hong Kong listing at top of range, raises over $3 billion
news.summary: China-headquartered Luxshare Precision Industry said on Tuesday it had priced its Hong ‌Kong listing at the top end of ‌its marketed range, raising about HK$24.27 billion ($3.09 billion). Here are some of ​the details: • The Shenzhen-listed Apple supplier said the offer price was determined at HK$63.28 per H-share, and would sell 383.5 million shares. Luxshare wrote in its ‌prospectus that the proceeds ⁠would be used to expand manufacturing capacity in automotive and consumer electronics, fund ⁠AI-driven factory upgrades, pursue acquisitions, repay debt and support working capital.
